---
title: "H-G06_final-project"
author: "Ishrar Islam"
date: "2026-04-15"
output:
  html_document:
    df_print: default
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    number_sections: true
---

## Introduction

This report presents a regression-based data science project using the Ames Housing dataset from Kaggle.  
The objective is to explore the dataset structure, understand feature types, visualize distributions and relationships, and prepare the data for later preprocessing and modeling.

## Dataset Source

**Dataset:** House Prices: Advanced Regression Techniques  
**Source:** Kaggle  
**Link:** https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/house-prices-advanced-regression-techniques/overview

## Project Workflow

This report is organized according to the project requirement: Data Collection, Data Exploration (EDA), Data Preprocessing, Modeling, and Evaluation.  
At this stage, the report includes the completed work for Data Collection and Data Exploration, and opens the Data Preprocessing section for the next part.

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# Global chunk options for cleaner report output
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.width = 10,
  fig.height = 6
)
```

## Package Setup

## Required Packages

The following chunk prepares the packages needed for data loading, manipulation, visualization, and exploratory analysis.  
The goal is to make the report reproducible on any machine without manual package setup.

```{r package-setup}
# List of required packages
packages <- c(
  "dplyr", "readr", "infotheo", "ggplot2", "scales",
  "tidyr", "GGally", "e1071", "reshape2", "VIM", "curl"
)

# Remove duplicates just in case
packages <- unique(packages)

# Install only missing packages
installed_packages <- rownames(installed.packages())
missing_packages <- packages[!(packages %in% installed_packages)]

if (length(missing_packages) > 0) {
  install.packages(missing_packages)
}

# Load all packages
invisible(lapply(packages, library, character.only = TRUE))
```

## Helper Function

A small helper function is defined below to compute the mode of a vector after excluding missing values.  
This function may be useful later during preprocessing when categorical imputation is needed.

```{r helper-function}
# Helper function to calculate the mode of a vector
get_mode <- function(v) {
  v <- v[!is.na(v)]
  uniqv <- unique(v)
  uniqv[which.max(tabulate(match(v, uniqv)))]
}
```

# Data Collection

### Objective of This Section

This section imports the dataset automatically using a public link.  
No local file path is used, so the code can run on any machine without modification.

## Dataset Loading

The following chunk reads the Ames Housing training dataset from a public Google Drive download URL.

```{r data-loading}
# Load the dataset into R using a public Google Drive download link
# Dataset source:
# Kaggle - House Prices: Advanced Regression Techniques
# https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/house-prices-advanced-regression-techniques/overview
# Original drive link:
# https://drive.google.com/file/d/1WsdHhc8rYF7VaRmTROoKBToeenVGkEkb/view?usp=drive_link
# Direct download link is used so the code runs automatically

# This dataset contains residential house information from Ames, Iowa.
# The target variable is SalePrice.
# It includes both categorical and numerical features, making it suitable for regression.

url <- "https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=1WsdHhc8rYF7VaRmTROoKBToeenVGkEkb"
dataset <- read_csv(url, show_col_types = FALSE)

# Show the first few rows to confirm successful loading
head(dataset)
```

# Data Exploration (EDA)

### Overview of the EDA Section

This section studies the dataset from several angles: overall structure, feature types, distributions, correlations, missing values, outliers, and duplicate rows.  
The goal is to understand the data before any preprocessing or modeling is applied.

## Dataset Shape, Types, and Summary Statistics

### Basic Dataset Shape

This subsection checks the size of the dataset by counting rows and columns.

```{r dataset-shape}
# Display the overall shape of the dataset
cat("Dataset Shape:\n")
cat("Number of rows    :", nrow(dataset), "\n")
cat("Number of columns :", ncol(dataset), "\n\n")
cat("Shape of dataset:", nrow(dataset), "rows ×", ncol(dataset), "columns")
```

### Structure and Data Types

This subsection inspects the structure of the dataset and creates a simple overview table showing the R data type, missing value count, and number of unique values for each feature.

```{r structure-and-types}
# Display the structure of the dataset
cat("Structure of the dataset:\n")
glimpse(dataset)

# Create a simple overview table for all columns
feature_overview <- data.frame(
  Feature = names(dataset),
  R_Type = sapply(dataset, function(x) class(x)[1]),
  Missing_Values = sapply(dataset, function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  Unique_Values = sapply(dataset, dplyr::n_distinct)
)

cat("\nFeature overview:\n")
print(feature_overview)

cat("\nCount of R data types:\n")
print(table(feature_overview$R_Type))
```

### Summary Statistics

This subsection provides both a general summary and a separate summary for numeric features, including mean, median, standard deviation, minimum, and maximum.

```{r summary-statistics}
# General summary for the full dataset
cat("\nOverall summary using summary():\n")
summary(dataset)

# Separate summary statistics for numeric columns
numeric_summary <- dataset %>%
  select(where(is.numeric)) %>%
  summarise(
    across(
      everything(),
      list(
        Mean   = ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
        Median = ~ median(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
        SD     = ~ sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
        Min    = ~ min(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
        Max    = ~ max(.x, na.rm = TRUE)
      )
    )
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = everything(),
    names_to = c("Feature", "Statistic"),
    names_pattern = "(.+)_(Mean|Median|SD|Min|Max)$",
    values_to = "Value"
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = Statistic, values_from = Value)

cat("\nNumeric summary statistics:\n")
print(numeric_summary)
```

## Identify Feature Types

### Feature-Type Logic

This subsection separates the identifier, target variable, numeric variables, and categorical variables.  
It also handles a few columns that are stored as numeric but behave more like categories in interpretation.

```{r feature-type-preparation}
# Define important feature groups
identifier_features <- c("Id")
target_feature <- c("SalePrice")

# These are stored as numeric originally, but they behave like categories
numeric_but_categorical <- c("MSSubClass", "MoSold", "YrSold")

# Convert numeric-but-categorical columns to factor type
for (col in numeric_but_categorical) {
  if (col %in% names(dataset)) {
    dataset[[col]] <- as.factor(dataset[[col]])
  }
}
```

### Numeric and Categorical Feature Matrices

This subsection creates separate tables for numeric and categorical features so they can be inspected more clearly.

```{r feature-matrices}
# Create lists of numeric and categorical features
numeric_features <- names(dataset)[sapply(dataset, is.numeric)]
numeric_features <- setdiff(numeric_features, "Id")   # Id is only an identifier

categorical_features <- names(dataset)[sapply(dataset, function(x) is.character(x))]

# Build a numeric feature summary table
numeric_feature_matrix <- data.frame(
  Feature = numeric_features,
  Missing_Values = sapply(dataset[numeric_features], function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  Unique_Values = sapply(dataset[numeric_features], dplyr::n_distinct),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Build a categorical feature summary table
categorical_feature_matrix <- data.frame(
  Feature = categorical_features,
  Missing_Values = sapply(dataset[categorical_features], function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  Unique_Values = sapply(dataset[categorical_features], dplyr::n_distinct),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

cat("Numeric Feature Matrix:\n")
print(numeric_feature_matrix)

cat("\nCategorical Feature Matrix:\n")
print(categorical_feature_matrix)
```

### Analytical Feature-Type Table

This subsection creates a final analytical feature-type table that labels each column according to its role in the project.

```{r analytical-feature-types}
# Create a final feature-type table
feature_types <- data.frame(Feature = names(dataset)) %>%
  mutate(
    Feature_Type = case_when(
      Feature %in% identifier_features ~ "Identifier",
      Feature %in% target_feature ~ "Target Variable (Continuous Numeric)",
      Feature %in% numeric_but_categorical ~ "Numeric but Categorical",
      Feature %in% categorical_features ~ "Categorical",
      Feature %in% numeric_features ~ "Numeric",
      TRUE ~ "Other"
    ),
    R_Type = sapply(dataset[Feature], function(x) class(x)[1])
  )

cat("\nIdentified feature types:\n")
print(feature_types)

cat("\nCount of analytical feature types:\n")
print(table(feature_types$Feature_Type))
```

## Visualize Distributions and Correlations

### Why These Visuals Are Used

This subsection explores the distribution of numeric and categorical variables and studies relationships among features.  
Histograms and boxplots help understand spread and outliers, bar charts show category frequencies, and correlation/scatter plots reveal relationships with the target.

### Histograms for Numeric Features

This chunk generates a histogram for each numeric feature.

```{r histograms-numeric, fig.height=5, fig.width=8}
# Create histograms for all numeric features
for (col in numeric_features) {
  x <- dataset[[col]]
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]

  if (length(x) > 0) {
    p <- ggplot(dataset, aes(x = .data[[col]])) +
      geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
      labs(
        title = paste("Histogram of", col),
        x = col,
        y = "Frequency"
      ) +
      theme_minimal()

    print(p)
  }
}
```

### Boxplots for Numeric Features

This chunk generates a boxplot for each numeric feature to inspect spread and possible outliers.

```{r boxplots-numeric, fig.height=5, fig.width=8}
# Create boxplots for all numeric features
for (col in numeric_features) {
  x <- dataset[[col]]
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]

  if (length(x) > 0) {
    p <- ggplot(dataset, aes(y = .data[[col]])) +
      geom_boxplot(fill = "tomato", color = "black", alpha = 0.7, outlier.color = "blue") +
      labs(
        title = paste("Boxplot of", col),
        y = col
      ) +
      theme_minimal()

    print(p)
  }
}
```

### Bar Charts for Categorical Features

This chunk generates a bar chart for each categorical feature to show the frequency of categories.

```{r barplots-categorical, fig.height=5, fig.width=8}
# Create bar charts for all categorical features
for (col in categorical_features) {
  plot_data <- dataset %>%
    filter(!is.na(.data[[col]])) %>%
    count(.data[[col]], name = "Count") %>%
    arrange(desc(Count))

  if (nrow(plot_data) > 0) {
    names(plot_data)[1] <- "Category"

    p <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = reorder(Category, Count), y = Count)) +
      geom_col(fill = "darkgreen", color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
      coord_flip() +
      labs(
        title = paste("Bar Chart of", col),
        x = col,
        y = "Count"
      ) +
      theme_minimal()

    print(p)
  }
}
```

### Correlation Heatmap for Numeric Features

This chunk computes the pairwise correlation matrix for numeric variables and visualizes it as a heatmap.

```{r correlation-heatmap, fig.height=10, fig.width=11}
# Select numeric data for correlation analysis
numeric_data_for_corr <- dataset %>%
  select(all_of(numeric_features))

# Compute correlation matrix using pairwise complete observations
cor_matrix <- cor(numeric_data_for_corr, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

# Convert matrix to long format for plotting
cor_long <- reshape2::melt(cor_matrix)

# Plot the correlation heatmap
ggplot(cor_long, aes(x = Var1, y = Var2, fill = value)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "blue",
    mid = "white",
    high = "red",
    midpoint = 0,
    limits = c(-1, 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Correlation Heatmap of Numeric Features",
    x = "",
    y = "",
    fill = "Correlation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5, size = 8),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8)
  )
```

### Correlation Table with the Target Variable

This chunk ranks numeric predictors by their absolute correlation with `SalePrice`.

```{r saleprice-correlation-table}
# Define the target variable and the list of numeric predictors
target <- "SalePrice"
numeric_predictors <- setdiff(numeric_features, target)

# Build a correlation table with SalePrice
saleprice_cor_table <- data.frame(
  Feature = numeric_predictors,
  Correlation_With_SalePrice = sapply(numeric_predictors, function(col) {
    cor(dataset[[col]], dataset[[target]], use = "complete.obs")
  })
)

saleprice_cor_table <- saleprice_cor_table %>%
  mutate(Abs_Correlation = abs(Correlation_With_SalePrice)) %>%
  arrange(desc(Abs_Correlation))

cat("\nCorrelation of numeric features with SalePrice:\n")
print(saleprice_cor_table)
```

### Scatter Plots of `SalePrice` vs Numeric Predictors

This chunk visualizes the relationship between the target and each numeric predictor using scatter plots with fitted linear trend lines.

```{r scatterplots-saleprice, fig.height=5, fig.width=8}
# Create scatter plots for SalePrice against all numeric predictors
for (col in numeric_predictors) {
  p <- ggplot(dataset, aes(x = .data[[col]], y = .data[[target]])) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "darkblue") +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
    labs(
      title = paste("Scatter Plot of", target, "vs", col),
      x = col,
      y = target
    ) +
    theme_minimal()

  print(p)
}
```

### Boxplots of `SalePrice` by Categorical Features

This chunk compares the distribution of `SalePrice` across all categorical features.

```{r saleprice-by-category, fig.height=5, fig.width=8}
# Create boxplots of SalePrice grouped by each categorical feature
for (col in categorical_features) {
  plot_data <- dataset %>%
    filter(!is.na(.data[[col]]), !is.na(.data[[target]])) %>%
    mutate(temp_category = reorder(as.character(.data[[col]]), .data[[target]], FUN = median))

  if (nrow(plot_data) > 0) {
    p <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = temp_category, y = .data[[target]])) +
      geom_boxplot(fill = "orange", color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
      coord_flip() +
      scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
      labs(
        title = paste("Boxplot of SalePrice by", col),
        x = col,
        y = "SalePrice"
      ) +
      theme_minimal()

    print(p)
  }
}
```

##  Detect Missing Values, Outliers, and Duplicate Rows

### Missing-Value Analysis

This subsection calculates the number and percentage of missing values for every column and also lists only the columns where missing values are present.

```{r missing-values-summary}
# Calculate missing values for all columns
missing_summary <- data.frame(
  Feature = names(dataset),
  Missing_Count = sapply(dataset, function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  Total_Rows = nrow(dataset),
  Missing_Percentage = round((sapply(dataset, function(x) sum(is.na(x))) / nrow(dataset)) * 100, 2),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Sort columns by missing count
missing_summary <- missing_summary %>%
  arrange(desc(Missing_Count))

cat("Missing values summary for all columns:\n")
print(missing_summary)

# Show only columns that actually have missing values
missing_only <- missing_summary %>%
  filter(Missing_Count > 0)

cat("\nColumns with missing values only:\n")
print(missing_only)
```

### Outlier Detection Using IQR

This subsection detects outliers in numeric columns using the standard IQR rule based on Q1, Q3, and 1.5 × IQR.

```{r outlier-summary}
# Initialize an empty data frame to store outlier summaries
outlier_summary <- data.frame()

# Detect outliers for each numeric feature using the IQR method
for (col in numeric_features) {
  x <- dataset[[col]]
  x_non_na <- x[!is.na(x)]

  # Skip the column if all values are missing
  if (length(x_non_na) == 0) next

  # Compute Q1, Q3, IQR, and bounds
  Q1 <- quantile(x_non_na, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
  Q3 <- quantile(x_non_na, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  IQR_value <- IQR(x_non_na, na.rm = TRUE)

  lower_bound <- Q1 - 1.5 * IQR_value
  upper_bound <- Q3 + 1.5 * IQR_value

  # Count outliers and compute their percentage
  outlier_count <- sum(x_non_na < lower_bound | x_non_na > upper_bound, na.rm = TRUE)
  outlier_percentage <- round((outlier_count / length(x_non_na)) * 100, 2)

  # Store the summary for this feature
  temp <- data.frame(
    Feature = col,
    Q1 = as.numeric(Q1),
    Q3 = as.numeric(Q3),
    IQR = as.numeric(IQR_value),
    Lower_Bound = as.numeric(lower_bound),
    Upper_Bound = as.numeric(upper_bound),
    Outlier_Count = outlier_count,
    Outlier_Percentage = outlier_percentage,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )

  outlier_summary <- rbind(outlier_summary, temp)
}

# Arrange features by number of outliers
outlier_summary <- outlier_summary %>%
  arrange(desc(Outlier_Count))

cat("\nOutlier summary for numeric columns using IQR method:\n")
print(outlier_summary)
```

```{r outlier-count}
# Show only columns with detected outliers
outlier_only <- outlier_summary %>%
  filter(Outlier_Count > 0)

cat("\nNumeric columns with detected outliers only:\n")
print(outlier_only)
```

### Detailed Outlier Rows

This subsection stores the actual outlier rows for each numeric feature so they can be inspected individually later.

```{r outlier-details}
# Create a list to store row-wise outlier details for each numeric column
outlier_details_list <- list()

for (col in numeric_features) {
  x <- dataset[[col]]

  # Compute IQR-based bounds while ignoring missing values
  Q1 <- quantile(x, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
  Q3 <- quantile(x, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  IQR_value <- IQR(x, na.rm = TRUE)

  lower_bound <- Q1 - 1.5 * IQR_value
  upper_bound <- Q3 + 1.5 * IQR_value

  # Extract rows that fall outside the lower/upper bounds
  outlier_rows <- dataset %>%
    filter(!is.na(.data[[col]]) & (.data[[col]] < lower_bound | .data[[col]] > upper_bound)) %>%
    select(Id, all_of(col))

  if (nrow(outlier_rows) > 0) {
    outlier_details_list[[col]] <- outlier_rows
  }
}

cat("\nOutlier details list created.\n")
cat("Use outlier_details_list[['SalePrice']] or outlier_details_list[['GrLivArea']] to inspect specific columns.\n")

# Print the list if full output is desired
print(outlier_details_list)
```

### Duplicate-Row Check

This subsection checks whether the dataset contains fully duplicated rows.

```{r duplicate-rows}
# Check duplicate rows in the whole dataset
duplicate_row_flags <- duplicated(dataset)
duplicate_row_count <- sum(duplicate_row_flags)

cat("Total duplicate rows in the dataset:", duplicate_row_count, "\n")
```

# Data Preprocessing

### Overview of the Next Step

The next stage of the project is data preprocessing.  
Based on the project requirement, this section will handle missing values and outliers, encode categorical variables, standardize or normalize numerical variables, perform feature engineering, and reduce skewness where necessary.

##  Handle Missing Values and Outliers

## Structural Missing Values: Justified Replacement (Part 1)

In this section, structural missing values are handled only after checking supporting evidence from related columns in the same row.  
The goal is to show that these missing values represent **absence of the feature**, not unknown data entry errors.

For each feature group below, the report first shows some sample rows, then checks whether the missing rows consistently match the related zero-valued columns.  
If the pattern is confirmed, the missing categorical values are replaced with `"None"` and the structural numeric values are replaced with `0`.

### Garage Features

For garage-related fields, the assumption is that if the following columns are missing in the same row:

- `GarageType`
- `GarageYrBlt`
- `GarageFinish`
- `GarageQual`
- `GarageCond`

and the same row also has:

- `GarageCars = 0`
- `GarageArea = 0`

then that row most likely represents a house with **no garage**.

```{r garage-justification-sample, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Show sample rows where all key garage descriptive columns are missing
# and inspect whether GarageCars and GarageArea are both zero

garage_missing_rows <- dataset %>%
  filter(
    is.na(GarageType),
    is.na(GarageYrBlt),
    is.na(GarageFinish),
    is.na(GarageQual),
    is.na(GarageCond)
  ) %>%
  select(
    Id, GarageType, GarageYrBlt, GarageFinish, GarageQual, GarageCond,
    GarageCars, GarageArea
  )

cat("Sample rows for garage-related missing values:\n")
print(garage_missing_rows %>% slice_head(n = 40))
```

```{r garage-justification-summary, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Count how many rows have all garage-descriptive columns missing
# and among them how many also have GarageCars = 0 and GarageArea = 0

garage_all_missing_idx <- with(
  dataset,
  is.na(GarageType) &
    is.na(GarageYrBlt) &
    is.na(GarageFinish) &
    is.na(GarageQual) &
    is.na(GarageCond)
)

garage_zero_support_idx <- with(
  dataset,
  garage_all_missing_idx &
    GarageCars == 0 &
    GarageArea == 0
)

garage_summary <- data.frame(
  Measure = c(
    "Rows where all 5 garage descriptive fields are missing",
    "Rows where all 5 garage descriptive fields are missing AND GarageCars = 0 AND GarageArea = 0",
    "Rows where garage descriptive fields are missing but the zero-value pattern does NOT hold"
  ),
  Count = c(
    sum(garage_all_missing_idx, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(garage_zero_support_idx, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(garage_all_missing_idx, na.rm = TRUE) - sum(garage_zero_support_idx, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)

cat("Garage justification summary:\n")
print(garage_summary)
```

```{r garage-fill-structural-missing, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# If the pattern holds, fill garage categorical missing values with "None"
# and the structural garage year with 0 for the justified rows

garage_categorical_cols <- c("GarageType", "GarageFinish", "GarageQual", "GarageCond")

for (col in garage_categorical_cols) {
  if (is.factor(dataset[[col]]) && !("None" %in% levels(dataset[[col]]))) {
    levels(dataset[[col]]) <- c(levels(dataset[[col]]), "None")
  }
  dataset[[col]][garage_zero_support_idx & is.na(dataset[[col]])] <- "None"
}

dataset$GarageYrBlt[garage_zero_support_idx & is.na(dataset$GarageYrBlt)] <- 0

garage_fill_check <- data.frame(
  Feature = c("GarageType", "GarageYrBlt", "GarageFinish", "GarageQual", "GarageCond"),
  Missing_After = c(
    sum(is.na(dataset$GarageType)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$GarageYrBlt)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$GarageFinish)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$GarageQual)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$GarageCond))
  )
)

cat("Garage columns after justified filling:\n")
print(garage_fill_check)
```

### Fireplace Quality

For fireplace quality, the idea is simple: if `FireplaceQu` is missing and `Fireplaces = 0`, then the row most likely means the house has **no fireplace**.

```{r fireplace-justification-sample, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Show sample rows where FireplaceQu is missing
# and inspect whether Fireplaces is zero in the same rows

fireplace_missing_rows <- dataset %>%
  filter(is.na(FireplaceQu)) %>%
  select(Id, Fireplaces, FireplaceQu)

cat("Sample rows for fireplace-related missing values:\n")
print(fireplace_missing_rows %>% slice_head(n = 40))
```

```{r fireplace-justification-summary, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Count how many rows have FireplaceQu missing
# and among them how many also have Fireplaces = 0

fireplace_missing_idx <- is.na(dataset$FireplaceQu)
fireplace_zero_support_idx <- fireplace_missing_idx & dataset$Fireplaces == 0

fireplace_summary <- data.frame(
  Measure = c(
    "Rows where FireplaceQu is missing",
    "Rows where FireplaceQu is missing AND Fireplaces = 0",
    "Rows where FireplaceQu is missing but Fireplaces is NOT 0"
  ),
  Count = c(
    sum(fireplace_missing_idx, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(fireplace_zero_support_idx, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(fireplace_missing_idx, na.rm = TRUE) - sum(fireplace_zero_support_idx, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)

cat("Fireplace justification summary:\n")
print(fireplace_summary)
```

```{r fireplace-fill-structural-missing, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Fill FireplaceQu with "None" only for rows justified as having no fireplace

if (is.factor(dataset$FireplaceQu) && !("None" %in% levels(dataset$FireplaceQu))) {
  levels(dataset$FireplaceQu) <- c(levels(dataset$FireplaceQu), "None")
}

dataset$FireplaceQu[fireplace_zero_support_idx & is.na(dataset$FireplaceQu)] <- "None"

cat("Remaining missing values in FireplaceQu after justified filling:\n")
print(sum(is.na(dataset$FireplaceQu)))
```

### Pool Quality

For pool quality, if `PoolQC` is missing and `PoolArea = 0`, then the row most likely means the house has **no pool**.

```{r pool-justification-sample, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Show sample rows where PoolQC is missing
# and inspect whether PoolArea is zero in the same rows

pool_missing_rows <- dataset %>%
  filter(is.na(PoolQC)) %>%
  select(Id, PoolArea, PoolQC)

cat("Sample rows for pool-related missing values:\n")
print(pool_missing_rows %>% slice_head(n = 40))
```

```{r pool-justification-summary, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Count how many rows have PoolQC missing
# and among them how many also have PoolArea = 0

pool_missing_idx <- is.na(dataset$PoolQC)
pool_zero_support_idx <- pool_missing_idx & dataset$PoolArea == 0

pool_summary <- data.frame(
  Measure = c(
    "Rows where PoolQC is missing",
    "Rows where PoolQC is missing AND PoolArea = 0",
    "Rows where PoolQC is missing but PoolArea is NOT 0"
  ),
  Count = c(
    sum(pool_missing_idx, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(pool_zero_support_idx, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(pool_missing_idx, na.rm = TRUE) - sum(pool_zero_support_idx, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)

cat("Pool justification summary:\n")
print(pool_summary)
```

```{r pool-fill-structural-missing, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Fill PoolQC with "None" only for rows justified as having no pool

if (is.factor(dataset$PoolQC) && !("None" %in% levels(dataset$PoolQC))) {
  levels(dataset$PoolQC) <- c(levels(dataset$PoolQC), "None")
}

dataset$PoolQC[pool_zero_support_idx & is.na(dataset$PoolQC)] <- "None"

cat("Remaining missing values in PoolQC after justified filling:\n")
print(sum(is.na(dataset$PoolQC)))
```

### Miscellaneous Feature

For miscellaneous features, if `MiscFeature` is missing and `MiscVal = 0`, then the row most likely means the house has **no miscellaneous feature recorded**.

```{r miscfeature-justification-sample, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Show sample rows where MiscFeature is missing
# and inspect whether MiscVal is zero in the same rows

misc_missing_rows <- dataset %>%
  filter(is.na(MiscFeature)) %>%
  select(Id, MiscFeature, MiscVal)

cat("Sample rows for miscellaneous feature missing values:\n")
print(misc_missing_rows %>% slice_head(n = 40))
```

```{r miscfeature-justification-summary, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Count how many rows have MiscFeature missing
# and among them how many also have MiscVal = 0

misc_missing_idx <- is.na(dataset$MiscFeature)
misc_zero_support_idx <- misc_missing_idx & dataset$MiscVal == 0

misc_summary <- data.frame(
  Measure = c(
    "Rows where MiscFeature is missing",
    "Rows where MiscFeature is missing AND MiscVal = 0",
    "Rows where MiscFeature is missing but MiscVal is NOT 0"
  ),
  Count = c(
    sum(misc_missing_idx, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(misc_zero_support_idx, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(misc_missing_idx, na.rm = TRUE) - sum(misc_zero_support_idx, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)

cat("MiscFeature justification summary:\n")
print(misc_summary)
```

```{r miscfeature-fill-structural-missing, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Fill MiscFeature with "None" only for rows justified as having no misc feature

if (is.factor(dataset$MiscFeature) && !("None" %in% levels(dataset$MiscFeature))) {
  levels(dataset$MiscFeature) <- c(levels(dataset$MiscFeature), "None")
}

dataset$MiscFeature[misc_zero_support_idx & is.na(dataset$MiscFeature)] <- "None"

cat("Remaining missing values in MiscFeature after justified filling:\n")
print(sum(is.na(dataset$MiscFeature)))
```

### Alley and Fence

For `Alley` and `Fence`, there is no strong numeric companion variable that can be used for row-wise proof in the same way as garage, fireplace, pool, or miscellaneous features.  
However, by variable meaning:

- `Alley` describes the type of alley access, so a missing value reasonably indicates **no alley access**
- `Fence` describes fence quality, so a missing value reasonably indicates **no fence**

Therefore, these missing values are treated as structural absence and filled with `"None"`.

```{r alley-fence-before-fill, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Check missing values before filling Alley and Fence

alley_fence_before <- data.frame(
  Feature = c("Alley", "Fence"),
  Missing_Before = c(
    sum(is.na(dataset$Alley)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$Fence))
  )
)

cat("Missing values before filling Alley and Fence:\n")
print(alley_fence_before)
```

```{r alley-fence-fill-structural-missing, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Fill Alley and Fence with "None" because missing values indicate
# absence of alley access or absence of a fence

for (col in c("Alley", "Fence")) {
  if (is.factor(dataset[[col]]) && !("None" %in% levels(dataset[[col]]))) {
    levels(dataset[[col]]) <- c(levels(dataset[[col]]), "None")
  }
  dataset[[col]][is.na(dataset[[col]])] <- "None"
}
```

```{r alley-fence-after-fill, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Check missing values after filling Alley and Fence

alley_fence_after <- data.frame(
  Feature = c("Alley", "Fence"),
  Missing_After = c(
    sum(is.na(dataset$Alley)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$Fence))
  )
)

cat("Missing values after filling Alley and Fence:\n")
print(alley_fence_after)
```



### Basement Features: Justified Structural Missing Values (37-Row Group)

Basement-related missing values are slightly more complex than the other structural-missing cases.  
In this dataset, four basement descriptive columns have either 37 or 38 missing values:

- `BsmtQual`
- `BsmtCond`
- `BsmtExposure`
- `BsmtFinType1`
- `BsmtFinType2`

A row-wise justification is used here.  
If a row has all key basement area variables equal to zero, then the missing basement descriptive fields likely indicate that the house has **no basement**, rather than unknown data.

In this subsection, only the **37-row structural no-basement group** is handled.  
Any extra inconsistent row will be left for later separate treatment.

```{r bsmt-justification-37row-sample, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Show sample rows where the basement appears to be structurally absent
# based on zero basement-area values and missing basement descriptive fields

bsmt_no_basement_rows <- dataset %>%
  filter(
    TotalBsmtSF == 0,
    BsmtFinSF1 == 0,
    BsmtFinSF2 == 0,
    BsmtUnfSF == 0,
    is.na(BsmtQual),
    is.na(BsmtCond),
    is.na(BsmtFinType1)
  ) %>%
  select(
    Id,
    BsmtQual, BsmtCond, BsmtExposure, BsmtFinType1, BsmtFinType2,
    BsmtFinSF1, BsmtFinSF2, BsmtUnfSF, TotalBsmtSF,
    BsmtFullBath, BsmtHalfBath
  )

cat("Sample rows from the 37-row structural no-basement group:\n")
print(bsmt_no_basement_rows %>% slice_head(n = 40))
```

```{r bsmt-justification-37row-summary, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Identify the 37-row structural no-basement group
# These rows have all basement size variables equal to zero
# and key basement descriptive fields missing

bsmt_structural_idx <- with(
  dataset,
  TotalBsmtSF == 0 &
    BsmtFinSF1 == 0 &
    BsmtFinSF2 == 0 &
    BsmtUnfSF == 0 &
    is.na(BsmtQual) &
    is.na(BsmtCond) &
    is.na(BsmtFinType1)
)

# Count how many rows match this structural no-basement pattern
# and compare it against the missing-value counts of basement descriptive columns

bsmt_summary_37 <- data.frame(
  Measure = c(
    "Rows in the structural no-basement group",
    "Missing count in BsmtQual",
    "Missing count in BsmtCond",
    "Missing count in BsmtFinType1",
    "Missing count in BsmtExposure",
    "Missing count in BsmtFinType2"
  ),
  Count = c(
    sum(bsmt_structural_idx, na.rm = TRUE),
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtQual)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtCond)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtFinType1)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtExposure)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtFinType2))
  )
)

cat("Summary of the 37-row structural basement pattern:\n")
print(bsmt_summary_37)
```

```{r bsmt-fill-37row-structural-missing, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Fill basement descriptive columns with "None"
# only for the justified 37-row no-basement group

bsmt_categorical_cols <- c(
  "BsmtQual",
  "BsmtCond",
  "BsmtExposure",
  "BsmtFinType1",
  "BsmtFinType2"
)

for (col in bsmt_categorical_cols) {
  
  # If the column is a factor, add "None" as a valid level first
  if (is.factor(dataset[[col]]) && !("None" %in% levels(dataset[[col]]))) {
    levels(dataset[[col]]) <- c(levels(dataset[[col]]), "None")
  }
  
  # Fill only the rows in the 37-row justified structural group
  dataset[[col]][bsmt_structural_idx & is.na(dataset[[col]])] <- "None"
}
```

```{r bsmt-after-37row-fill, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Check remaining missing values after filling only the structural 37-row group

bsmt_after_37_fill <- data.frame(
  Feature = c("BsmtQual", "BsmtCond", "BsmtExposure", "BsmtFinType1", "BsmtFinType2"),
  Missing_After = c(
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtQual)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtCond)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtExposure)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtFinType1)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtFinType2))
  )
)

cat("Remaining missing values in basement descriptive columns after filling the 37-row structural group:\n")
print(bsmt_after_37_fill)
```

```{r bsmt-leftover-extra-rows, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Show the remaining rows that are still missing basement descriptive values
# after the 37-row structural group has been handled

bsmt_remaining_rows <- dataset %>%
  filter(
    is.na(BsmtExposure) | is.na(BsmtFinType2) |
    is.na(BsmtQual) | is.na(BsmtCond) | is.na(BsmtFinType1)
  ) %>%
  select(
    Id,
    BsmtQual, BsmtCond, BsmtExposure, BsmtFinType1, BsmtFinType2,
    BsmtFinSF1, BsmtFinSF2, BsmtUnfSF, TotalBsmtSF,
    BsmtFullBath, BsmtHalfBath
  )

cat("Remaining basement-missing rows after handling the 37-row structural group:\n")
print(bsmt_remaining_rows)
```

### Masonry Veneer Missing Values (`MasVnrType` and `MasVnrArea`)

`MasVnrType` and `MasVnrArea` each contain a very small number of missing values.  
However, these two variables describe the same masonry veneer feature and are strongly related.  
Because of this dependency, simple independent imputation using mode for `MasVnrType` and median for `MasVnrArea` could create unrealistic or unsupported type–area combinations.

In addition, the missingness pattern could not be strongly justified as structural absence in the same way as garage, fireplace, pool, or basement features.  
Therefore, instead of forcing imputation, the affected rows are removed from the training dataset. Since only 8 rows are affected, this removal is unlikely to have any meaningful impact on model performance.

```{r masvnr-missing-rows-sample, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Show the rows where MasVnrType or MasVnrArea is missing
# This helps inspect the affected observations before removal

masvnr_missing_rows <- dataset %>%
  filter(is.na(MasVnrType) | is.na(MasVnrArea)) %>%
  select(Id, MasVnrType, MasVnrArea)

cat("Rows with missing values in MasVnrType or MasVnrArea:\n")
print(masvnr_missing_rows)
```

```{r masvnr-missing-summary, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Count the number of affected rows and the percentage of the dataset

masvnr_missing_idx <- is.na(dataset$MasVnrType) | is.na(dataset$MasVnrArea)

masvnr_summary <- data.frame(
  Measure = c(
    "Rows with missing MasVnrType",
    "Rows with missing MasVnrArea",
    "Rows affected in either column",
    "Percentage of total dataset"
  ),
  Value = c(
    sum(is.na(dataset$MasVnrType)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$MasVnrArea)),
    sum(masvnr_missing_idx),
    round(sum(masvnr_missing_idx) / nrow(dataset) * 100, 2)
  )
)

cat("Summary of missing values in masonry veneer variables:\n")
print(masvnr_summary)
```

```{r masvnr-drop-rows, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Store dataset size before dropping rows
rows_before_masvnr_drop <- nrow(dataset)

# Remove rows where either MasVnrType or MasVnrArea is missing
dataset <- dataset %>%
  filter(!(is.na(MasVnrType) | is.na(MasVnrArea)))

# Store dataset size after dropping rows
rows_after_masvnr_drop <- nrow(dataset)

# Create a summary of the row removal
masvnr_drop_summary <- data.frame(
  Measure = c(
    "Rows before dropping",
    "Rows removed",
    "Rows after dropping"
  ),
  Value = c(
    rows_before_masvnr_drop,
    rows_before_masvnr_drop - rows_after_masvnr_drop,
    rows_after_masvnr_drop
  )
)

cat("Dataset size before and after removing masonry veneer missing rows:\n")
print(masvnr_drop_summary)
```

```{r masvnr-check-after-drop, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Verify that no missing values remain in MasVnrType and MasVnrArea

masvnr_after_drop_check <- data.frame(
  Feature = c("MasVnrType", "MasVnrArea"),
  Missing_After = c(
    sum(is.na(dataset$MasVnrType)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$MasVnrArea))
  )
)

cat("Missing values in MasVnrType and MasVnrArea after row removal:\n")
print(masvnr_after_drop_check)
```

### Remove the Remaining Special-Case Missing Rows

After handling structural missing values and removing the 8 rows with unresolved masonry veneer missingness, only a very small number of special-case rows remain with missing values in basement-related fields and `Electrical`.

These remaining rows are not ideal candidates for direct imputation:

- the basement rows are inconsistent cases rather than clean structural-missing cases
- the `Electrical` column has only one missing row

Since these remaining cases affect only a very small fraction of the dataset, they are removed instead of forcing unsupported imputations.

```{r drop-remaining-special-missing-rows-sample, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Show the remaining rows with missing basement-related values or missing Electrical
# before dropping them from the dataset

remaining_special_missing_rows <- dataset %>%
  filter(
    is.na(BsmtExposure) |
    is.na(BsmtFinType2) |
    is.na(Electrical)
  ) %>%
  select(
    Id,
    BsmtQual, BsmtCond, BsmtExposure, BsmtFinType1, BsmtFinType2,
    BsmtFinSF1, BsmtFinSF2, BsmtUnfSF, TotalBsmtSF,
    BsmtFullBath, BsmtHalfBath,
    Electrical
  )

cat("Remaining special-case rows with basement or Electrical missing values:\n")
print(remaining_special_missing_rows)
```

```{r drop-remaining-special-missing-rows-summary, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Count how many rows are affected before dropping them

remaining_special_missing_idx <- with(
  dataset,
  is.na(BsmtExposure) |
    is.na(BsmtFinType2) |
    is.na(Electrical)
)

remaining_special_missing_summary <- data.frame(
  Measure = c(
    "Rows with missing BsmtExposure",
    "Rows with missing BsmtFinType2",
    "Rows with missing Electrical",
    "Total rows to be removed"
  ),
  Value = c(
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtExposure)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtFinType2)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$Electrical)),
    sum(remaining_special_missing_idx)
  )
)

cat("Summary of the remaining special-case missing rows:\n")
print(remaining_special_missing_summary)
```

```{r drop-remaining-special-missing-rows, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Store the dataset size before dropping rows
rows_before_special_drop <- nrow(dataset)

# Remove the remaining special-case rows
dataset <- dataset %>%
  filter(
    !(
      is.na(BsmtExposure) |
      is.na(BsmtFinType2) |
      is.na(Electrical)
    )
  )

# Store the dataset size after dropping rows
rows_after_special_drop <- nrow(dataset)

# Summarize the row removal
special_drop_summary <- data.frame(
  Measure = c(
    "Rows before dropping",
    "Rows removed",
    "Rows after dropping"
  ),
  Value = c(
    rows_before_special_drop,
    rows_before_special_drop - rows_after_special_drop,
    rows_after_special_drop
  )
)

cat("Dataset size before and after removing the remaining special-case rows:\n")
print(special_drop_summary)
```

```{r check-after-special-drop, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Verify that no missing values remain in the dropped special-case columns

special_drop_check <- data.frame(
  Feature = c("BsmtExposure", "BsmtFinType2", "Electrical"),
  Missing_After = c(
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtExposure)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$BsmtFinType2)),
    sum(is.na(dataset$Electrical))
  )
)

cat("Missing values after removing the remaining special-case rows:\n")
print(special_drop_check)
```

### Impute `LotFrontage` Using Neighborhood-Wise Median

`LotFrontage` still contains missing values after the other missing-value issues have been resolved.  
Since frontage is related to property location and lot characteristics, it is more reasonable to impute it using the median within each `Neighborhood` rather than using a single global value directly.

The median is preferred over the mean because it is more robust to extreme values and skewness.  
After neighborhood-wise imputation, any remaining missing values are filled using the overall dataset median as a fallback.

```{r lotfrontage-before-imputation, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Check the number of missing LotFrontage values before imputation

lotfrontage_before <- data.frame(
  Feature = "LotFrontage",
  Missing_Before = sum(is.na(dataset$LotFrontage))
)

cat("Missing values in LotFrontage before imputation:\n")
print(lotfrontage_before)
```

```{r lotfrontage-imputation, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Save the overall median as a fallback value
overall_lotfrontage_median <- median(dataset$LotFrontage, na.rm = TRUE)

# Fill missing LotFrontage values using the median within each Neighborhood
dataset <- dataset %>%
  group_by(Neighborhood) %>%
  mutate(
    LotFrontage = ifelse(
      is.na(LotFrontage),
      median(LotFrontage, na.rm = TRUE),
      LotFrontage
    )
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# If any LotFrontage values are still missing, fill them using the overall median
dataset$LotFrontage[is.na(dataset$LotFrontage)] <- overall_lotfrontage_median
```

```{r lotfrontage-after-imputation, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Verify that LotFrontage no longer contains missing values

lotfrontage_after <- data.frame(
  Feature = "LotFrontage",
  Missing_After = sum(is.na(dataset$LotFrontage))
)

cat("Missing values in LotFrontage after imputation:\n")
print(lotfrontage_after)
```


```{r missing-values-summary2}
# Calculate missing values for all columns after handeling al missing values
missing_summary <- data.frame(
  Feature = names(dataset),
  Missing_Count = sapply(dataset, function(x) sum(is.na(x))),
  Total_Rows = nrow(dataset),
  Missing_Percentage = round((sapply(dataset, function(x) sum(is.na(x))) / nrow(dataset)) * 100, 2),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Sort columns by missing count
missing_summary <- missing_summary %>%
  arrange(desc(Missing_Count))

cat("Missing values summary for all columns:\n")
print(missing_summary)
```



### Handle Outliers Selectively
### Outlier Detection Using IQR after filling missing data

This subsection detects outliers in numeric columns using the standard IQR rule based on Q1, Q3, and 1.5 × IQR.

```{r outlier-summary-2}
# Initialize an empty data frame to store outlier summaries
outlier_summary <- data.frame()

# Detect outliers for each numeric feature using the IQR method
for (col in numeric_features) {
  x <- dataset[[col]]
  x_non_na <- x[!is.na(x)]

  # Skip the column if all values are missing
  if (length(x_non_na) == 0) next

  # Compute Q1, Q3, IQR, and bounds
  Q1 <- quantile(x_non_na, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
  Q3 <- quantile(x_non_na, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  IQR_value <- IQR(x_non_na, na.rm = TRUE)

  lower_bound <- Q1 - 1.5 * IQR_value
  upper_bound <- Q3 + 1.5 * IQR_value

  # Count outliers and compute their percentage
  outlier_count <- sum(x_non_na < lower_bound | x_non_na > upper_bound, na.rm = TRUE)
  outlier_percentage <- round((outlier_count / length(x_non_na)) * 100, 2)

  # Store the summary for this feature
  temp <- data.frame(
    Feature = col,
    Q1 = as.numeric(Q1),
    Q3 = as.numeric(Q3),
    IQR = as.numeric(IQR_value),
    Lower_Bound = as.numeric(lower_bound),
    Upper_Bound = as.numeric(upper_bound),
    Outlier_Count = outlier_count,
    Outlier_Percentage = outlier_percentage,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )

  outlier_summary <- rbind(outlier_summary, temp)
}

# Arrange features by number of outliers
outlier_summary <- outlier_summary %>%
  arrange(desc(Outlier_Count))

cat("\nOutlier summary for numeric columns using IQR method:\n")
print(outlier_summary)
```



### Why Only Selected Columns Are Treated

Although the IQR-based outlier summary identified many numeric columns as containing outliers, not all of them are suitable for direct outlier treatment.

Many numeric features in the Ames Housing dataset have one or more of the following properties:

- they contain a very large number of zero values
- they are count-based variables, such as number of rooms, fireplaces, or garages
- they are year-based variables
- they are structurally related to other variables, meaning direct modification could distort the original meaning of the data

Because of this, a global outlier-handling approach was not applied to all numeric columns.  
Instead, outlier treatment was limited to `LotArea` and `LotFrontage`, as these are continuous, positive, and more naturally suited to transformation.

Rather than capping or removing observations, a `log1p()` transformation is used.  
This reduces right-skewness and weakens the influence of extreme large values while preserving all rows.

### Outlier Count Before Transformation

The following chunk calculates the IQR-based outlier count for `LotArea` and `LotFrontage` before transformation.

```{r lotarea-lotfrontage-outliers-before-after, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Function to calculate IQR-based outlier count for one numeric vector
get_outlier_count <- function(x) {
  x_non_na <- x[!is.na(x)]
  
  Q1 <- quantile(x_non_na, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
  Q3 <- quantile(x_non_na, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  IQR_value <- IQR(x_non_na, na.rm = TRUE)
  
  lower_bound <- Q1 - 1.5 * IQR_value
  upper_bound <- Q3 + 1.5 * IQR_value
  
  outlier_count <- sum(x_non_na < lower_bound | x_non_na > upper_bound, na.rm = TRUE)
  
  data.frame(
    Q1 = as.numeric(Q1),
    Q3 = as.numeric(Q3),
    IQR = as.numeric(IQR_value),
    Lower_Bound = as.numeric(lower_bound),
    Upper_Bound = as.numeric(upper_bound),
    Outlier_Count = outlier_count,
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
}

# Outlier summary before transformation
lotarea_before <- get_outlier_count(dataset$LotArea)
lotfrontage_before <- get_outlier_count(dataset$LotFrontage)

outlier_before_summary <- rbind(
  cbind(Feature = "LotArea", lotarea_before),
  cbind(Feature = "LotFrontage", lotfrontage_before)
)

cat("Outlier summary before log transformation:\n")
print(outlier_before_summary)
```


### Why Only Selected Columns Are Treated

Although the IQR-based outlier summary identified many numeric columns as containing outliers, not all of them are suitable for direct outlier treatment.

Many numeric features in the Ames Housing dataset have one or more of the following properties:

- they contain a very large number of zero values
- they are count-based variables, such as number of rooms, fireplaces, or garages
- they are year-based variables
- they are structurally related to other variables, meaning direct modification could distort the original meaning of the data

Because of this, a global outlier-handling approach was not applied to all numeric columns.  
Instead, outlier treatment was limited to `LotArea` and `LotFrontage`, as these are continuous, positive, and more naturally suited to transformation.

Rather than capping or removing observations, a `log1p()` transformation is used.  
This reduces right-skewness and weakens the influence of extreme large values while preserving all rows.

### Outlier Count Before Transformation

The following chunk calculates the IQR-based outlier count for `LotArea` and `LotFrontage` before transformation.

```{r lotarea-lotfrontage-outliers-before, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# Outlier summary before transformation
lotarea_before <- get_outlier_count(dataset$LotArea)
lotfrontage_before <- get_outlier_count(dataset$LotFrontage)

outlier_before_summary <- rbind(
  cbind(Feature = "LotArea", lotarea_before),
  cbind(Feature = "LotFrontage", lotfrontage_before)
)

cat("Outlier summary before log transformation:\n")
print(outlier_before_summary)
```

### Apply Log Transformation

The following chunk applies `log1p()` transformation to both `LotArea` and `LotFrontage`.

`log1p(x)` means `log(1 + x)`, which is useful for positive variables and remains safe when values are zero.

```{r lotarea-lotfrontage-log-transform, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Apply log1p transformation to reduce the influence of large extreme values
# and reduce right-skewness while keeping all rows

dataset$LotArea_log <- log1p(dataset$LotArea)
dataset$LotFrontage_log <- log1p(dataset$LotFrontage)

cat("Log transformation applied to LotArea and LotFrontage.\n")
```

### Outlier Count After Transformation

This chunk recalculates the IQR-based outlier count after transformation to see whether the transformation reduced the number of detected outliers.

```{r lotarea-lotfrontage-outliers-after, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Outlier summary after transformation
lotarea_after <- get_outlier_count(dataset$LotArea_log)
lotfrontage_after <- get_outlier_count(dataset$LotFrontage_log)

outlier_after_summary <- rbind(
  cbind(Feature = "LotArea_log", lotarea_after),
  cbind(Feature = "LotFrontage_log", lotfrontage_after)
)

cat("Outlier summary after log transformation:\n")
print(outlier_after_summary)
```

### Compare Before and After

This final chunk compares the outlier count before and after transformation for the two selected variables.

```{r lotarea-lotfrontage-outlier-comparison, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Compare outlier counts before and after transformation

outlier_comparison <- data.frame(
  Feature = c("LotArea", "LotFrontage"),
  Outliers_Before = c(
    lotarea_before$Outlier_Count,
    lotfrontage_before$Outlier_Count
  ),
  Outliers_After = c(
    lotarea_after$Outlier_Count,
    lotfrontage_after$Outlier_Count
  )
)

cat("Comparison of outlier counts before and after transformation:\n")
print(outlier_comparison)
```


```{r sqrt-transform-apply, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Apply square root transformation to the two selected variables
# New columns are created so the original variables remain unchanged

dataset$LotArea_sqrt <- sqrt(dataset$LotArea)
dataset$LotFrontage_sqrt <- sqrt(dataset$LotFrontage)

cat("Square root transformation applied to LotArea and LotFrontage.\n")
```

```{r sqrt-outliers-after, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Calculate and save outlier summaries for the transformed variables

lotarea_after_sqrt <- get_outlier_count(dataset$LotArea_sqrt)
lotfrontage_after_sqrt <- get_outlier_count(dataset$LotFrontage_sqrt)

sqrt_outlier_after_summary <- rbind(
  cbind(Feature = "LotArea_sqrt", lotarea_after_sqrt),
  cbind(Feature = "LotFrontage_sqrt", lotfrontage_after_sqrt)
)

cat("Outlier summary after square root transformation:\n")
print(sqrt_outlier_after_summary)
```

```{r sqrt-outlier-comparison, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Compare outlier counts before and after square root transformation

sqrt_outlier_comparison <- data.frame(
  Feature = c("LotArea", "LotFrontage"),
  Outliers_Before = c(
    lotarea_before$Outlier_Count,
    lotfrontage_before$Outlier_Count
  ),
  Outliers_After = c(
    lotarea_after_sqrt$Outlier_Count,
    lotfrontage_after_sqrt$Outlier_Count
  )
)

cat("Comparison of outlier counts before and after square root transformation:\n")
print(sqrt_outlier_comparison)
```



##  Encode Ordinal Categorical Variables


```{r inspect-nominal-unique-values, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Store the unique current values for each nominal column
# The current values are used because preprocessing may already have changed some categories
# for example, adding "None" for structural missing values

unique_values <- list()
cat_col <- names(dataset)[sapply(dataset, function(x) is.character(x))]

for (col in cat_col) {
  unique_values[[col]] <- sort(unique(as.character(dataset[[col]])))
}
cat("Categorical features.\n")
print(cat_col)
cat("Unique values for nominal columns have been collected.\n")
cat("Use nominal_unique_values[['Neighborhood']] or nominal_unique_values[['GarageType']] to inspect a specific column.\n")

print(unique_values)
```
### Why Label Encoding Is Used for These Columns

The following categorical columns are treated as **ordinal** because their categories have a meaningful rank or progression.  
For these variables, simple one-hot encoding would ignore the natural order among categories.

Therefore, **label encoding** is used, where:

- the **worst / lowest** category is encoded as `0`
- better categories receive progressively higher values
- the **best / highest** category receives the largest value

For columns that now contain `"None"` after missing-value handling, `"None"` is treated as the lowest possible category.

### Ordinal Ranking Rules

The ordinal ranking used in this project is:

- `LotShape`: `IR3 < IR2 < IR1 < Reg`
- `LandSlope`: `Sev < Mod < Gtl`
- `ExterQual`: `Fa < TA < Gd < Ex`
- `ExterCond`: `Po < Fa < TA < Gd < Ex`
- `BsmtQual`: `None < Fa < TA < Gd < Ex`
- `BsmtCond`: `None < Po < Fa < TA < Gd`
- `BsmtExposure`: `None < No < Mn < Av < Gd`
- `BsmtFinType1`: `None < Unf < LwQ < Rec < BLQ < ALQ < GLQ`
- `BsmtFinType2`: `None < Unf < LwQ < Rec < BLQ < ALQ < GLQ`
- `HeatingQC`: `Po < Fa < TA < Gd < Ex`
- `KitchenQual`: `Fa < TA < Gd < Ex`
- `Functional`: `Sev < Maj2 < Maj1 < Mod < Min2 < Min1 < Typ`
- `FireplaceQu`: `None < Po < Fa < TA < Gd < Ex`
- `GarageFinish`: `None < Unf < RFn < Fin`
- `GarageQual`: `None < Po < Fa < TA < Gd < Ex`
- `GarageCond`: `None < Po < Fa < TA < Gd < Ex`
- `PavedDrive`: `N < P < Y`
- `PoolQC`: `None < Fa < Gd < Ex`
- `Fence`: `None < MnWw < GdWo < MnPrv < GdPrv`

### Define the Ordinal Mappings

This chunk creates the label-encoding rules for each ordinal feature.  
The lowest-quality category starts from `0`, and better categories receive larger integers.

```{r define-ordinal-mappings, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Create a named list of ordinal mappings
# Each mapping assigns the lowest/worst category to 0
# and higher/better categories to larger integers

ordinal_mappings <- list(
  LotShape = c("IR3" = 0, "IR2" = 1, "IR1" = 2, "Reg" = 3),
  LandSlope = c("Sev" = 0, "Mod" = 1, "Gtl" = 2),
  
  ExterQual = c("Fa" = 0, "TA" = 1, "Gd" = 2, "Ex" = 3),
  ExterCond = c("Po" = 0, "Fa" = 1, "TA" = 2, "Gd" = 3, "Ex" = 4),
  
  BsmtQual = c("None" = 0, "Fa" = 1, "TA" = 2, "Gd" = 3, "Ex" = 4),
  BsmtCond = c("None" = 0, "Po" = 1, "Fa" = 2, "TA" = 3, "Gd" = 4),
  BsmtExposure = c("None" = 0, "No" = 1, "Mn" = 2, "Av" = 3, "Gd" = 4),
  BsmtFinType1 = c("None" = 0, "Unf" = 1, "LwQ" = 2, "Rec" = 3, "BLQ" = 4, "ALQ" = 5, "GLQ" = 6),
  BsmtFinType2 = c("None" = 0, "Unf" = 1, "LwQ" = 2, "Rec" = 3, "BLQ" = 4, "ALQ" = 5, "GLQ" = 6),
  
  HeatingQC = c("Po" = 0, "Fa" = 1, "TA" = 2, "Gd" = 3, "Ex" = 4),
  KitchenQual = c("Fa" = 0, "TA" = 1, "Gd" = 2, "Ex" = 3),
  Functional = c("Sev" = 0, "Maj2" = 1, "Maj1" = 2, "Mod" = 3, "Min2" = 4, "Min1" = 5, "Typ" = 6),
  
  FireplaceQu = c("None" = 0, "Po" = 1, "Fa" = 2, "TA" = 3, "Gd" = 4, "Ex" = 5),
  
  GarageFinish = c("None" = 0, "Unf" = 1, "RFn" = 2, "Fin" = 3),
  GarageQual = c("None" = 0, "Po" = 1, "Fa" = 2, "TA" = 3, "Gd" = 4, "Ex" = 5),
  GarageCond = c("None" = 0, "Po" = 1, "Fa" = 2, "TA" = 3, "Gd" = 4, "Ex" = 5),
  
  PavedDrive = c("N" = 0, "P" = 1, "Y" = 2),
  
  PoolQC = c("None" = 0, "Fa" = 1, "Gd" = 2, "Ex" = 3),
  
  Fence = c("None" = 0, "MnWw" = 1, "GdWo" = 2, "MnPrv" = 3, "GdPrv" = 4)
)

cat("Ordinal mappings have been defined.\n")
print(names(ordinal_mappings))
```

### Apply Label Encoding to the Ordinal Columns

This chunk applies the mappings using a loop.  
A new encoded column is created for each feature using the suffix `_ord`.

```{r apply-ordinal-encoding, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Apply ordinal encoding using the mapping list
# The original columns are kept unchanged
# New encoded columns are created with suffix "_ord"

for (col in names(ordinal_mappings)) {
  
  # Create the new encoded column name
  new_col_name <- paste0(col, "_ord")
  
  # Convert values using the defined mapping
  dataset[[new_col_name]] <- unname(ordinal_mappings[[col]][as.character(dataset[[col]])])
}

cat("Ordinal label encoding completed.\n")
```

### Check the Encoded Columns

This chunk verifies the encoding by printing the original distinct values and their encoded forms for each ordinal column.

```{r verify-ordinal-encoding, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Create a verification list showing original values and encoded values
ordinal_encoding_check <- list()

for (col in names(ordinal_mappings)) {
  
  encoded_col <- paste0(col, "_ord")
  
  check_table <- data.frame(
    Original_Value = sort(unique(as.character(dataset[[col]]))),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  
  check_table$Encoded_Value <- unname(ordinal_mappings[[col]][check_table$Original_Value])
  
  ordinal_encoding_check[[col]] <- check_table
}

cat("Ordinal encoding check created.\n")
cat("Use ordinal_encoding_check[['LotShape']] or ordinal_encoding_check[['BsmtQual']] to inspect a specific feature.\n")

print(ordinal_encoding_check)
```

### Summary of the Newly Created Ordinal Columns

This chunk lists the newly created ordinal-encoded variables.

```{r list-encoded-ordinal-columns, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# List all newly created ordinal encoded columns
ordinal_encoded_columns <- paste0(names(ordinal_mappings), "_ordinal")

cat("New ordinal encoded columns added to the dataset:\n")
print(ordinal_encoded_columns)
```
### Encode Nominal Categorical Variables

### Why One-Hot Encoding Is Used for These Columns

The following categorical columns are treated as **nominal** because their values do **not** have a natural ranking or progression.  
For such variables, label encoding would incorrectly impose an artificial numeric order. Therefore, **one-hot encoding** is used instead.

In one-hot encoding:

- each category is converted into a separate binary column
- a value of `1` means the row belongs to that category
- a value of `0` means the row does not belong to that category

The original nominal columns are kept unchanged, and the new dummy columns are added directly to the main dataset.

### Define the Nominal Columns

This chunk defines the list of nominal categorical features to be one-hot encoded.  
The list is based on the earlier feature-type analysis.

```{r define-nominal-columns, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Define the nominal categorical columns
# These variables do not have a meaningful order

nominal_features <- c(
  "MSZoning",
  "Utilities",
  "Street",
  "Alley",
  "LandContour",
  "LotConfig",
  "Neighborhood",
  "Condition1",
  "Condition2",
  "BldgType",
  "HouseStyle",
  "RoofStyle",
  "RoofMatl",
  "Exterior1st",
  "Exterior2nd",
  "MasVnrType",
  "Foundation",
  "Heating",
  "CentralAir",
  "Electrical",
  "GarageType",
  "MiscFeature",
  "SaleType",
  "SaleCondition"
)

# Keep only the columns that actually exist in the current dataset
# This makes the code safer if any column was removed or renamed earlier
nominal_features <- intersect(nominal_features, names(dataset))

cat("Nominal columns selected for one-hot encoding:\n")
print(nominal_features)
```

### Apply One-Hot Encoding to the Nominal Columns

This chunk applies one-hot encoding using a loop.  
For each nominal variable, separate dummy columns are created and added to the main dataset.

```{r apply-onehot-encoding, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Initialize a list to store the names of all new dummy columns
nominal_dummy_columns <- list()

# Apply one-hot encoding to each nominal feature
for (col in nominal_features) {
  
  # Convert the column to factor to ensure proper category handling
  temp_factor <- as.factor(dataset[[col]])
  
  # Create a model matrix with one binary column per category
  # The "-1" removes the intercept so that all categories get their own columns
  dummy_matrix <- model.matrix(~ temp_factor - 1)
  
  # Create cleaner and more informative column names
  # make.names() is used to ensure valid R column names
  cleaned_levels <- make.names(levels(temp_factor))
  new_dummy_names <- paste0(col, "_", cleaned_levels)
  
  # Assign the cleaned names
  colnames(dummy_matrix) <- new_dummy_names
  
  # Add the dummy columns to the main dataset
  dataset <- cbind(dataset, as.data.frame(dummy_matrix))
  
  # Store the names of the created dummy columns
  nominal_dummy_columns[[col]] <- new_dummy_names
}

cat("One-hot encoding completed for nominal categorical variables.\n")
```

### Verify the Newly Created Dummy Columns

This chunk prints the dummy-column names created for each nominal feature.

```{r verify-onehot-columns, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Print the dummy columns created for each nominal feature

cat("Dummy columns created for each nominal feature:\n")
print(nominal_dummy_columns)
```

### Count the Total Number of Dummy Columns Created

This chunk summarizes how many one-hot encoded columns were generated.

```{r count-onehot-columns, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Count the total number of one-hot encoded columns created

total_nominal_dummy_columns <- sum(sapply(nominal_dummy_columns, length))

onehot_summary <- data.frame(
  Measure = c(
    "Number of nominal columns encoded",
    "Total number of dummy columns created"
  ),
  Value = c(
    length(nominal_features),
    total_nominal_dummy_columns
  )
)

cat("Summary of one-hot encoding:\n")
print(onehot_summary)
```

### Final Check

This chunk shows the updated dataset shape after adding all nominal dummy variables.

```{r dataset-shape-after-onehot, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Show the dataset size after one-hot encoding

cat("Updated dataset shape after one-hot encoding:\n")
cat("Number of rows    :", nrow(dataset), "\n")
cat("Number of columns :", ncol(dataset), "\n")
```



##  Normalize / Standardize Numerical Features

### Why Only Selected Numeric Features Are Scaled

Scaling is applied only to the final numeric predictor features that are suitable for transformation.  
It is not applied to:

- `Id`, because it is only an identifier
- `SalePrice`, because it is the target variable
- `MSSubClass`, `MoSold`, and `YrSold`, because they are treated as categorical in meaning
- transformed duplicate versions such as `LotArea_sqrt`, `LotFrontage_sqrt`, `LotArea_log`, and `LotFrontage_log`, because they are not being used as final main numeric predictors

In this section, both **standardization** and **normalization** are created and stored inside the main dataset as new columns:

- columns ending with `_std` = standardized versions
- columns ending with `_norm` = normalized versions

The original variables remain unchanged.

### Select the Numeric Features to Scale

This chunk creates the numeric feature list after excluding identifier, target, categorical-style numeric fields, and duplicate transformed columns.

```{r select-numeric-features-for-scaling, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Columns that should not be scaled
exclude_from_scaling <- c(
  "SalePrice",
  "MSSubClass",
  "MoSold",
  "YrSold",
  "LotArea_sqrt",
  "LotFrontage_sqrt",
  "LotArea_log",
  "LotFrontage_log"
)



# Keep only the numeric features that should actually be scaled
numeric_features2 <- setdiff(numeric_features, exclude_from_scaling)

cat("Numeric features selected for scaling:\n")
print(numeric_features2)
```

### Define Scaling Functions

This chunk defines two helper functions:

- one for z-score standardization
- one for min-max normalization

A safety check is included for constant-value columns.

```{r define-scaling-functions, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Function for z-score standardization
# Formula: (x - mean) / standard deviation
standardize_feature <- function(x) {
  if (sd(x, na.rm = TRUE) == 0) {
    return(rep(0, length(x)))
  } else {
    return((x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  }
}

# Function for min-max normalization
# Formula: (x - min) / (max - min)
normalize_feature <- function(x) {
  min_val <- min(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  max_val <- max(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  
  if ((max_val - min_val) == 0) {
    return(rep(0, length(x)))
  } else {
    return((x - min_val) / (max_val - min_val))
  }
}
```

### Add Standardized and Normalized Columns to the Main Dataset

This chunk adds two new versions of each selected numeric feature directly into the main dataset:

- `<original_name>_std`
- `<original_name>_norm`

```{r add-scaled-columns-to-main-dataset, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Add standardized and normalized versions directly into the main dataset

for (col in numeric_features2) {
  
  # Create new standardized column name
  std_col_name <- paste0(col, "_std")
  
  # Create new normalized column name
  norm_col_name <- paste0(col, "_norm")
  
  # Add the transformed columns into the same dataset
  dataset[[std_col_name]] <- standardize_feature(dataset[[col]])
  dataset[[norm_col_name]] <- normalize_feature(dataset[[col]])
}

cat("Standardized and normalized columns have been added to the main dataset.\n")
```

### Verify the Standardized Columns

This chunk checks whether the standardized columns have mean close to 0 and standard deviation close to 1.

```{r verify-standardized-columns, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Create the list of standardized column names
standardized_columns <- paste0(numeric_features2, "_std")

# Check the mean and standard deviation of standardized columns
standardization_check <- data.frame(
  Feature = standardized_columns,
  Mean_After_Standardization = sapply(dataset[standardized_columns], function(x) round(mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), 4)),
  SD_After_Standardization = sapply(dataset[standardized_columns], function(x) round(sd(x, na.rm = TRUE), 4)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

cat("Verification of standardized columns:\n")
print(standardization_check)
```

### Verify the Normalized Columns

This chunk checks whether the normalized columns fall within the range \([0,1]\).

```{r verify-normalized-columns, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Create the list of normalized column names
normalized_columns <- paste0(numeric_features2, "_norm")

# Check the minimum and maximum of normalized columns
normalization_check <- data.frame(
  Feature = normalized_columns,
  Min_After_Normalization = sapply(dataset[normalized_columns], function(x) round(min(x, na.rm = TRUE), 4)),
  Max_After_Normalization = sapply(dataset[normalized_columns], function(x) round(max(x, na.rm = TRUE), 4)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

cat("Verification of normalized columns:\n")
print(normalization_check)
```

### Final Dataset Check

This final chunk shows the updated dataset shape after adding the new scaled columns.

```{r final-dataset-check-after-scaling, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
cat("Updated dataset shape after adding standardized and normalized columns:\n")
cat("Number of rows    :", nrow(dataset), "\n")
cat("Number of columns :", ncol(dataset), "\n")
```
##  Feature Engineering

### Objective of This Section
Feature engineering involves creating new variables from existing data to help the model better capture the underlying patterns that drive house prices. By combining related features, we can reduce dimensionality and provide the model with more "meaningful" signals as required by the project guidelines.


### Total Indoor Square Footage
Instead of treating the basement and above-ground living areas separately, we combine them into one TotalSF feature. This represents the total enclosed living space available to the buyer, which is often the single most important factor in determining property value

```{r fe1, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Create Total Indoor Square Footage
dataset <- dataset %>% 
  mutate(TotalSF = TotalBsmtSF + GrLivArea)

cat("Created TotalSF by combining TotalBsmtSF and GrLivArea.")
```

### Total Outdoor Square Footage
Houses in the Ames dataset have several porch and deck types (WoodDeckSF, OpenPorchSF, etc.). We combine these into a single TotalOutdoorSF variable. This reduces noise by focusing on the total usable outdoor area rather than the specific type of porch


```{r fe2, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Create Total Outdoor Square Footage
dataset <- dataset %>% 
  mutate(TotalOutdoorSF = WoodDeckSF + OpenPorchSF + EnclosedPorch + 
                          `3SsnPorch` + ScreenPorch)

cat("Created TotalOutdoorSF by combining all porch and deck areas.")
```

### Total Bathroom Count
A house's utility is highly dependent on the number of bathrooms. We follow standard real estate logic by counting a full bath as 1.0 and a half bath as 0.5. This provides a more precise continuous variable for the regression model.


```{r fe3, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Create Total Bathroom count
dataset <- dataset %>% 
  mutate(TotalBath = FullBath + (0.5 * HalfBath) + 
                     BsmtFullBath + (0.5 * BsmtHalfBath))

cat("Created TotalBath by combining all full and half baths.")
```
### Property Age and Years Since Remodel
The absolute YearBuilt is less important than how old the house was at the time it was sold. We also calculate the years since the last remodel, as homes with recent updates typically command a higher price premium.

```{r fe4, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Create HouseAge and YearsSinceRemodel
dataset <- dataset %>% 
  mutate(HouseAge = as.numeric(as.character(YrSold)) - YearBuilt,
         YearsSinceRemodel = as.numeric(as.character(YrSold)) - YearRemodAdd)

cat("Created HouseAge and YearsSinceRemodel based on the year sold.")
```

### Overall Property Score
This is an interaction term. By multiplying OverallQual (material/finish) and OverallCond (maintenance), we allow the model to capture the "synergy" between a well-built house that has also been well-maintained.

```{r fe5, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Create TotalHouseScore as an interaction term
dataset <- dataset %>% 
  mutate(TotalHouseScore = OverallQual * OverallCond)

cat("Created TotalHouseScore by multiplying Quality and Condition.")
```
### Luxury Amenities (Binary Flags)
Variables like PoolArea and Fireplaces have many zero values because these amenities are rare. We create binary flags (HasPool, HasFireplace) to tell the model directly whether these luxury features exist, simplifying the data signal.

```{r fe6, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Create binary flags for rare amenities
dataset <- dataset %>% 
  mutate(HasPool = ifelse(PoolArea > 0, 1, 0),
         HasFireplace = ifelse(Fireplaces > 0, 1, 0))

cat("Created binary flags for Pool and Fireplace presence.")
```
### Space Efficiency (Average Room Size)
By dividing the total living area by the number of rooms above ground, we create an AvgRoomArea feature. This helps the model distinguish between houses with many small rooms versus those with an open, spacious layout

```{r fe7, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Create Average Room Area
dataset <- dataset %>% 
  mutate(AvgRoomArea = ifelse(TotRmsAbvGrd > 0, GrLivArea / TotRmsAbvGrd, 0))

cat("Created AvgRoomArea to measure space efficiency.")
```
### Log Transformation of the Target Variable
To address the right-skewness of SalePrice observed in the EDA section, we apply a log transformation. This ensures the target variable follows a more normal distribution, which is a key assumption for the Linear Regression model.

```{r fe8, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Handle Skewness of the Target (SalePrice)
dataset$SalePrice_log <- log(dataset$SalePrice)

cat("Log transformation applied to SalePrice. SalePrice_log will be used for modeling.")
```

###Reasons for Dropping Original Columns
SalePrice: We created SalePrice_log to make the data more "normal," so the original price column is no longer needed for the math part of the model.  

TotalBsmtSF and GrLivArea: These two are already added together inside our new TotalSF column, so keeping them would be repeating the same information.  

Individual Bathroom Counts: FullBath, HalfBath, BsmtFullBath, and BsmtHalfBath are all combined into the single TotalBath variable.  

Building and Sale Years: YearBuilt, YearRemodAdd, and YrSold were only used to calculate the house age and remodel age; once we have HouseAge, the specific years are useless to the model.  

Porch and Deck Areas: All five individual porch types (Wood Deck, Open Porch, etc.) are now summed up in TotalOutdoorSF, making the original columns redundant.  

PoolArea and Fireplaces: We created simple "Yes/No" flags (HasPool and HasFireplace) to represent these, so the original area and count numbers are no longer necessary.  

Original LotArea and LotFrontage: Since we are using the log-transformed versions to fix skewness, we drop the originals so the model doesn't "double-count" the same land data.  

Id: This is just a row ID number and has nothing to do with how much a house is worth.


```{r fe9, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# 1. Create a copy for modeling
modeling_dataset <- dataset

# 2. List original columns to drop (The "Building Blocks")
# These have been replaced by engineered features like TotalSF, TotalBath, etc.
building_blocks <- c(
  "TotalBsmtSF", "GrLivArea",                                    # Replaced by TotalSF
  "FullBath", "HalfBath", "BsmtFullBath", "BsmtHalfBath",        # Replaced by TotalBath
  "YearBuilt", "YearRemodAdd", "YrSold",                        # Replaced by HouseAge/YearsSinceRemodel
  "WoodDeckSF", "OpenPorchSF", "EnclosedPorch", 
  "3SsnPorch", "ScreenPorch",                                    # Replaced by TotalOutdoorSF
  "PoolArea", "Fireplaces", "PoolQC", "FireplaceQu"                                       # Replaced by binary flags
)

# 3. List redundant transformations and identifiers to drop
redundant_cols <- c(
  "Id",                      # Non-predictive identifier
  "SalePrice",               # Replaced by SalePrice_log
  "LotArea", "LotFrontage" ,  # Replaced by LotArea_log and LotFrontage_log
  "LotArea_sqrt", "LotFrontage_sqrt"
)

# 4. Remove all Square Root and Normalized versions 
extra_transforms <- grep("(_norm|_sqrt)$", names(modeling_dataset), value = TRUE)
building_blocks_std <- paste0(building_blocks, "_std")
redundant_cols_std <- paste0(redundant_cols, "_std")

# 5. Execute the drop using any_of() to avoid "missing element" errors

modeling_dataset <- modeling_dataset %>% select(-any_of(building_blocks))
modeling_dataset <- modeling_dataset %>% select(-any_of(redundant_cols))
modeling_dataset <- modeling_dataset %>% select(-any_of(extra_transforms))
modeling_dataset <- modeling_dataset %>% select(-any_of(building_blocks_std))
modeling_dataset <- modeling_dataset %>% select(-any_of(redundant_cols_std))
modeling_dataset <- modeling_dataset %>% select(-any_of(numeric_features2))
modeling_dataset <- modeling_dataset %>% select(-any_of(cat_col))


# 6. Final Clean: Ensure all column names are valid for R models
names(modeling_dataset) <- make.names(names(modeling_dataset), unique = TRUE)

cat("Modeling dataset finalized.")
cat("Total rows    :", nrow(modeling_dataset))
cat("Total columns :", ncol(modeling_dataset))
```
### Main dataset column
Main dataset which has all column with out any droppping all the names of columns of main dataset 

```{r fe10, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Get all column names
column_names <- colnames(dataset)

# Print a numbered list of all columns so none are missed in the report
cat(paste0(1:length(column_names), ". ", column_names), sep = "\n")

cat("\nTotal Column Count:", ncol(dataset))
```



### Final dataset column
Total number of columns and all columns name for final dataset wich is ready fir model training

```{r fe11, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Get all column names
final_column_names <- colnames(modeling_dataset)

# Print a numbered list of all columns so none are missed in the report
cat(paste0(1:length(final_column_names), ". ", final_column_names), sep = "\n")

cat("\nTotal Column Count:", ncol(modeling_dataset))
```

##  Check Skewness of Numeric Columns

### Why Skewness Is Checked

Skewness measures the asymmetry of a numeric variable’s distribution.  
A skewness value near `0` suggests a roughly symmetric distribution, while larger positive or negative values indicate stronger skewness.

At this stage, skewness is checked for **all numeric columns** in the current dataset.  
This step is only for diagnosis and interpretation. It does not automatically mean that every skewed variable will be transformed.

### Compute Skewness for All Numeric Columns

This chunk calculates skewness for every numeric column in the dataset and sorts the result by the absolute size of skewness.

```{r skewness-all-numeric-columns, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Calculate skewness for each numeric column

# We exclude the target 'SalePrice' (if present) or its log version for this table

modeling_numeric_cols <- grep("(_std|_log)$", colnames(modeling_dataset), value = TRUE)
modeling_numeric_cols <- setdiff(modeling_numeric_cols, "SalePrice_log")

# na.rm = TRUE ensures missing values do not interfere
skewness_summary <- data.frame(
  Feature = modeling_numeric_cols,
  Skewness = sapply(modeling_dataset[modeling_numeric_cols], function(x) e1071::skewness(x, na.rm = TRUE, type = 2)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Add absolute skewness for easier ranking
skewness_summary$Abs_Skewness <- abs(skewness_summary$Skewness)

# Sort by highest absolute skewness
skewness_summary <- skewness_summary %>%
  arrange(desc(Abs_Skewness))

cat("Skewness summary for all numeric columns:\n")
print(skewness_summary)
```

### Identify Strongly Skewed Numeric Columns

This chunk filters the numeric columns whose absolute skewness is greater than `1`, which is a common rule-of-thumb threshold for strong skewness.

```{r strongly-skewed-numeric-columns, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Filter strongly skewed numeric columns using |skewness| > 1
strongly_skewed_numeric <- skewness_summary %>%
  filter(Abs_Skewness > 1)

cat("Strongly skewed numeric columns (|skewness| > 1):\n")
print(strongly_skewed_numeric)
```
### Elective Skewness Correction
Why These Features Were Chosen:
Continuous Area Signals: X1stFlrSF, MasVnrArea, and BsmtFinSF1 represent physical dimensions where even small changes can significantly impact price.  

Normalizing Distribution: These features exhibit high right-skewness. Applying a log transformation pulls extreme outliers closer to the mean, making the distribution more symmetric.  

Linear Model Performance: Linear Regression assumes a linear relationship between predictors and the target; log-transforming area features helps stabilize variance and linearize these relationships.  

Why Other Skewed Features (e.g., MiscVal, PoolArea) Were Excluded:
Sparsity Problem: Features like MiscVal and LowQualFinSF are mostly zeros (sparse data). Transforming a column that is 95% zero will not make it "Normal".

Binary Interpretation: For sparse features, a simple "Yes/No" binary flag (already created in Section 4.6) provides a much cleaner signal to the model than a skewed numeric value.
