Discrete Counts: Count variables (like KitchenAbvGr) are discrete. Log transformations are intended for continuous measurements, not small integer counts.


```{r strongly-skewed-numeric-columns-handeling, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Apply log1p transformation to replace original values
# log1p is used to safely handle zero values [log(1+x)]

# Create log-transformed versions of key area features
modeling_dataset$X1stFlrSF_log <- log1p(dataset$`1stFlrSF`)
modeling_dataset$MasVnrArea_log <- log1p(dataset$MasVnrArea)
modeling_dataset$BsmtFinSF1_log <- log1p(dataset$BsmtFinSF1)

cat("Created _log versions for X1stFlrSF, MasVnrArea, and BsmtFinSF1 to handle skewness.")

cat("Skewness handled for X1stFlrSF, MasVnrArea, and BsmtFinSF1 by replacing original values with log-transformed data.")

up_cols <- c("X1stFlrSF_log", "MasVnrArea_log", "BsmtFinSF1_log")
# na.rm = TRUE ensures missing values do not interfere
skewness_summary <- data.frame(
  Feature = up_cols,
  Skewness = sapply(modeling_dataset[up_cols], function(x) e1071::skewness(x, na.rm = TRUE, type = 2)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

# Add absolute skewness for easier ranking
skewness_summary$Abs_Skewness <- abs(skewness_summary$Skewness)

# Sort by highest absolute skewness
skewness_summary <- skewness_summary %>%
  arrange(desc(Abs_Skewness))

cat("Skewness summary for all numeric columns:\n")
print(skewness_summary)


del_cols <- c("X1stFlrSF_std", "MasVnrArea_std", "BsmtFinSF1_std")
modeling_dataset <- modeling_dataset %>% select(-any_of(del_cols))
```



# Modeling

### Selected Regression Models

Two regression models are used in this project:

- **Linear Regression** as the baseline model
- **Random Forest Regression** as the main model

The dataset is split into training and testing sets using the **80:20 rule**, and model performance is evaluated using:

- **RMSE** (Root Mean Squared Error)
- **MAE** (Mean Absolute Error)
- **R²** (Coefficient of Determination)

### Why These Models Were Chosen

Linear Regression is used as a simple and interpretable baseline model.  
Random Forest is used as the main model because it can capture nonlinear relationships and usually performs well on structured tabular regression data such as housing datasets.

##  Prepare the Final Modeling Dataset

This subsection prepares the dataset for modeling.

- `Id` is removed because it is only an identifier
- generated duplicate columns such as `_std`, `_norm`, `_log`, and `_sqrt` are excluded from modeling
- `SalePrice` is kept as the target variable

```{r prepare-modeling-dataset, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Make all column names valid for modeling
names(modeling_dataset) <- make.names(names(modeling_dataset), unique = TRUE)

cat("Final modeling dataset shape:\n")
cat("Number of rows    :", nrow(modeling_dataset), "\n")
cat("Number of columns :", ncol(modeling_dataset), "\n")
```

## Train-Test Split (80:20)

This subsection splits the data into training and testing sets.

```{r train-test-split, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(123)

# Create an 80:20 train-test split
train_index <- sample(
  x = seq_len(nrow(modeling_dataset)),
  size = floor(0.80 * nrow(modeling_dataset))
)

train_data <- modeling_dataset[train_index, ]
test_data  <- modeling_dataset[-train_index, ]

cat("Training set size :", nrow(train_data), "rows\n")
cat("Testing set size  :", nrow(test_data), "rows\n")
```

##  Define Evaluation Metrics

This chunk defines a small helper function to compute regression metrics.

```{r regression-metrics-function, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Function to calculate regression evaluation metrics
regression_metrics <- function(actual, predicted) {
  rmse <- sqrt(mean((actual - predicted)^2))
  mae  <- mean(abs(actual - predicted))
  r2   <- 1 - sum((actual - predicted)^2) / sum((actual - mean(actual))^2)
  
  data.frame(
    RMSE = rmse,
    MAE  = mae,
    R2   = r2
  )
}
```

##  Linear Regression Model

This subsection trains the baseline Linear Regression model and generates predictions on the test set.

Since one-hot encoded dummy columns were added for all nominal categories, the Linear Regression model is fitted **without intercept** (`- 1`) to avoid perfect multicollinearity caused by full dummy encoding.

```{r linear-regression-model, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Train the Linear Regression model
linear_model <- lm(SalePrice_log ~ . - 1, data = train_data)

# Predict SalePrice for the test set
linear_predictions <- predict(linear_model, newdata = test_data)

# Evaluate the Linear Regression model
linear_results <- regression_metrics(
  actual = test_data$SalePrice,
  predicted = linear_predictions
)

cat("Linear Regression performance:\n")
print(linear_results)
```

##  Random Forest Regression Model

This subsection trains the Random Forest model and generates predictions on the test set.

```{r random-forest-model, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(randomForest)
# Train the Random Forest model
rf_model <- randomForest(
  SalePrice_log ~ .,
  data = train_data,
  ntree = 500,
  importance = TRUE
)

# Predict SalePrice for the test set
rf_predictions <- predict(rf_model, newdata = test_data)

# Evaluate the Random Forest model
rf_results <- regression_metrics(
  actual = test_data$SalePrice,
  predicted = rf_predictions
)

cat("Random Forest performance:\n")
print(rf_results)
```

##  Compare Model Performance

This subsection combines the results of both models into one comparison table.

```{r compare-regression-models, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Combine the results of both models into a single comparison table
model_comparison <- rbind(
  cbind(Model = "Linear Regression", linear_results),
  cbind(Model = "Random Forest", rf_results)
)

cat("Comparison of model performance on the test set:\n")
print(model_comparison)
```
```{r tuning-package-setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Install and load required packages for tuning if needed

if (!require(MASS)) {
  install.packages("MASS")
  library(MASS)
}

if (!require(randomForest)) {
  install.packages("randomForest")
  library(randomForest)
}
```

##  Tune Linear Regression Using Stepwise AIC

### Create Inner Train-Validation Split for Tuning

This chunk creates an inner split inside the training data.  
The inner validation set is used only for tuning.

```{r inner-train-validation-split, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Create inner train-validation split from the training data
set.seed(456)

inner_train_index <- sample(
  x = seq_len(nrow(train_data)),
  size = floor(0.80 * nrow(train_data))
)

train_inner <- train_data[inner_train_index, ]
valid_inner <- train_data[-inner_train_index, ]

cat("Inner training set size   :", nrow(train_inner), "rows\n")
cat("Inner validation set size :", nrow(valid_inner), "rows\n")
```


### Why This Is Considered Tuning for Linear Regression

Ordinary Linear Regression does not have many standard hyperparameters like tree-based models.  
Therefore, tuning is done by selecting a better subset of predictors using **stepwise AIC**.

This procedure starts from the full model and removes or adds predictors to improve model quality according to the AIC criterion.

```{r tune-linear-regression-stepwise, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Fit the full Linear Regression model on the inner training set
# "-1" is used because full one-hot encoding was applied earlier
full_lm_inner <- lm(SalePrice_log ~ . - 1, data = train_inner)

# Tune the model using stepwise AIC
tuned_lm_inner <- MASS::stepAIC(
  full_lm_inner,
  direction = "both",
  trace = FALSE
)

cat("Stepwise AIC tuning for Linear Regression completed.\n")
cat("Final tuned Linear Regression formula:\n")
print(formula(tuned_lm_inner))
```

### Evaluate the Tuned Linear Regression on the Validation Set

```{r evaluate-tuned-linear-regression-validation, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Predict on the inner validation set
lm_valid_predictions <- predict(tuned_lm_inner, newdata = valid_inner)

# Evaluate validation performance
lm_valid_results <- regression_metrics(
  actual = valid_inner$SalePrice_log,
  predicted = lm_valid_predictions
)

cat("Validation performance of tuned Linear Regression:\n")
print(lm_valid_results)
```

### Refit the Tuned Linear Regression on the Full Training Set

This chunk refits the tuned formula using the full outer training data, then evaluates it on the final test set.

```{r final-tuned-linear-regression-test, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Refit the tuned Linear Regression model on the full outer training set
final_tuned_lm <- lm(
  formula(tuned_lm_inner),
  data = train_data
)

# Predict on the final test set
lm_test_predictions <- predict(final_tuned_lm, newdata = test_data)

# Evaluate final test performance
lm_tuned_results <- regression_metrics(
  actual = test_data$SalePrice_log,
  predicted = lm_test_predictions
)

cat("Final test performance of tuned Linear Regression:\n")
print(lm_tuned_results)
```

##  Tune Random Forest Using Grid Search

### Why These Hyperparameters Are Tuned

For Random Forest, the following hyperparameters are tuned:

- `mtry` = number of variables randomly tried at each split
- `ntree` = number of trees in the forest
- `nodesize` = minimum size of terminal nodes

A small grid is used so that tuning remains practical for a student project.

```{r random-forest-tuning-grid, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Determine number of predictors
p <- ncol(train_inner) - 1

# Create a small hyperparameter grid
mtry_grid <- unique(pmax(1, round(c(sqrt(p), p / 3, p / 2))))
ntree_grid <- c(300, 500)
nodesize_grid <- c(3, 5)

rf_tuning_grid <- expand.grid(
  mtry = mtry_grid,
  ntree = ntree_grid,
  nodesize = nodesize_grid
)

cat("Random Forest tuning grid:\n")
print(rf_tuning_grid)
```

### Run Random Forest Grid Search

```{r run-rf-grid-search, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Initialize an empty data frame to store tuning results
rf_tuning_results <- data.frame()

# Grid search over Random Forest hyperparameters
for (i in 1:nrow(rf_tuning_grid)) {
  
  current_mtry <- rf_tuning_grid$mtry[i]
  current_ntree <- rf_tuning_grid$ntree[i]
  current_nodesize <- rf_tuning_grid$nodesize[i]
  
  set.seed(123 + i)
  
  # Train Random Forest candidate model
  rf_candidate <- randomForest(
    SalePrice_log ~ .,
    data = train_inner,
    mtry = current_mtry,
    ntree = current_ntree,
    nodesize = current_nodesize,
    importance = TRUE
  )
  
  # Predict on validation set
  rf_valid_predictions <- predict(rf_candidate, newdata = valid_inner)
  
  # Evaluate validation performance
  rf_valid_metrics <- regression_metrics(
    actual = valid_inner$SalePrice,
    predicted = rf_valid_predictions
  )
  
  # Store result
  temp_result <- data.frame(
    mtry = current_mtry,
    ntree = current_ntree,
    nodesize = current_nodesize,
    RMSE = rf_valid_metrics$RMSE,
    MAE = rf_valid_metrics$MAE,
    R2 = rf_valid_metrics$R2
  )
  
  rf_tuning_results <- rbind(rf_tuning_results, temp_result)
}

cat("Random Forest tuning results on validation set:\n")
print(rf_tuning_results)
```

### Select the Best Random Forest Parameters

```{r select-best-rf-params, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Select the best hyperparameter combination
# Lower RMSE is the main criterion

best_rf_params <- rf_tuning_results %>%
  arrange(RMSE, MAE, desc(R2)) %>%
  slice(1)

cat("Best Random Forest hyperparameters:\n")
print(best_rf_params)
```

### Refit the Tuned Random Forest on the Full Training Set

```{r final-tuned-rf-test, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Train the final tuned Random Forest model on the full outer training set
final_tuned_rf <- randomForest(
  SalePrice_log ~ .,
  data = train_data,
  mtry = best_rf_params$mtry,
  ntree = best_rf_params$ntree,
  nodesize = best_rf_params$nodesize,
  importance = TRUE
)

# Predict on the final test set
rf_test_predictions <- predict(final_tuned_rf, newdata = test_data)

# Evaluate final test performance
rf_tuned_results <- regression_metrics(
  actual = test_data$SalePrice,
  predicted = rf_test_predictions
)

cat("Final test performance of tuned Random Forest:\n")
print(rf_tuned_results)
```

##  Compare the Tuned Models

```{r compare-tuned-models, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Compare the final tuned models on the test set
tuned_model_comparison <- rbind(
  cbind(Model = "Tuned Linear Regression", lm_tuned_results),
  cbind(Model = "Tuned Random Forest", rf_tuned_results)
)

cat("Comparison of tuned models on the test set:\n")
print(tuned_model_comparison)
```


#   Evaluation & Interpretation

The table above compares both models using:

- **RMSE**: lower is better
- **MAE**: lower is better
- **R²**: higher is better

The Linear Regression model serves as a baseline, while Random Forest is expected to perform better if the relationship between features and house prices is nonlinear.

```{r c1, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

cat("Linear Regression performance before tunning:\n")
print(linear_results)

cat("Linear Regression performance after tunning:\n")
print(lm_tuned_results)

```


```{r c2, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

cat("Random Forest performance before tuning:\n")
print(rf_results)

cat("Random Forest performance after tuning:\n")
print(rf_tuned_results)

```

##  Prepare Final Modeling Dataset

```{r model-package-setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Required packages for modeling and visualization
extra_packages <- c("randomForest", "MASS", "rpart", "rpart.plot")

installed_packages <- rownames(installed.packages())
missing_extra <- extra_packages[!(extra_packages %in% installed_packages)]

if (length(missing_extra) > 0) {
  install.packages(missing_extra)
}

invisible(lapply(extra_packages, library, character.only = TRUE))
```

##  Test Predictions and Final Result Table

```{r final-predictions-and-results, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

# Final comparison table
final_results_table <- rbind(
  cbind(Model = "Linear Regression - Normal", linear_results),
  cbind(Model = "Linear Regression - Tuned",  lm_tuned_results),
  cbind(Model = "Random Forest - Normal",    rf_results),
  cbind(Model = "Random Forest - Tuned",     rf_tuned_results)
)

cat("Final comparison of all models:\n")
print(final_results_table)
```

##  Create a Combined Prediction Data Frame for Plots

```{r build-plot-data, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
plot_results <- rbind(
  data.frame(Model = "Linear Regression - Normal",
             Actual = test_data$SalePrice_log,
             Predicted = linear_predictions),
  data.frame(Model = "Linear Regression - Tuned",
             Actual = test_data$SalePrice_log,
             Predicted = lm_test_predictions),
  data.frame(Model = "Random Forest - Normal",
             Actual = test_data$SalePrice_log,
             Predicted = rf_predictions),
  data.frame(Model = "Random Forest - Tuned",
             Actual = test_data$SalePrice_log,
             Predicted = rf_test_predictions)
)

plot_results$Residual <- plot_results$Actual - plot_results$Predicted
```

##  Mandatory Result Visuals for Regression

###  Actual vs Predicted Plot

```{r actual-vs-predicted-plot, fig.height=8, fig.width=11}
ggplot(plot_results, aes(x = Actual, y = Predicted)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~ Model, scales = "free") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Actual vs Predicted Values",
    x = "Actual SalePrice",
    y = "Predicted SalePrice"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

###  Residual vs Predicted Plot

```{r residual-vs-predicted-plot, fig.height=8, fig.width=11}
ggplot(plot_results, aes(x = Predicted, y = Residual)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  facet_wrap(~ Model, scales = "free") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Residual vs Predicted Plot",
    x = "Predicted SalePrice",
    y = "Residual"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

###  Residual Distribution

```{r residual-histogram-plot, fig.height=8, fig.width=11}
ggplot(plot_results, aes(x = Residual)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", color = "black", alpha = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ Model, scales = "free") +
  labs(
    title = "Residual Distribution by Model",
    x = "Residual",
    y = "Frequency"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

##  Linear Regression Visuals

###  Coefficient Plot for Normal Linear Regression

```{r lm-normal-coef-plot, fig.height=7, fig.width=10}
lm_coef_normal <- data.frame(
  Feature = names(coef(linear_model)),
  Coefficient = as.numeric(coef(linear_model))
) %>%
  filter(!is.na(Coefficient)) %>%
  mutate(AbsCoef = abs(Coefficient)) %>%
  arrange(desc(AbsCoef)) %>%
  slice(1:20)

ggplot(lm_coef_normal, aes(x = reorder(Feature, AbsCoef), y = Coefficient)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 20 Coefficients: Linear Regression - Normal",
    x = "Feature",
    y = "Coefficient"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

###  Coefficient Plot for Tuned Linear Regression

```{r lm-tuned-coef-plot, fig.height=7, fig.width=10}
lm_coef_tuned <- data.frame(
  Feature = names(coef(final_tuned_lm)),
  Coefficient = as.numeric(coef(final_tuned_lm))
) %>%
  filter(!is.na(Coefficient)) %>%
  mutate(AbsCoef = abs(Coefficient)) %>%
  arrange(desc(AbsCoef)) %>%
  slice(1:20)

ggplot(lm_coef_tuned, aes(x = reorder(Feature, AbsCoef), y = Coefficient)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 20 Coefficients: Linear Regression - Tuned",
    x = "Feature",
    y = "Coefficient"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

##  Random Forest Visuals

###  OOB Error Curve for Normal Random Forest

```{r rf-normal-oob-plot, fig.height=6, fig.width=9}
rf_oob_normal <- data.frame(
  Trees = 1:length(rf_model$mse),
  OOB_MSE = rf_model$mse
)

ggplot(rf_oob_normal, aes(x = Trees, y = OOB_MSE)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(
    title = "OOB Error Curve: Random Forest - Normal",
    x = "Number of Trees",
    y = "OOB Mean Squared Error"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

###  OOB Error Curve for Tuned Random Forest

```{r rf-tuned-oob-plot, fig.height=6, fig.width=9}
rf_oob_tuned <- data.frame(
  Trees = 1:length(final_tuned_rf$mse),
  OOB_MSE = final_tuned_rf$mse
)

ggplot(rf_oob_tuned, aes(x = Trees, y = OOB_MSE)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(
    title = "OOB Error Curve: Random Forest - Tuned",
    x = "Number of Trees",
    y = "OOB Mean Squared Error"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

###  Variable Importance Plot for Normal Random Forest

```{r rf-normal-importance-plot, fig.height=7, fig.width=10}
rf_imp_normal <- importance(rf_model)

rf_imp_normal_df <- data.frame(
  Feature = rownames(rf_imp_normal),
  Importance = rf_imp_normal[, if ("%IncMSE" %in% colnames(rf_imp_normal)) "%IncMSE" else 1]
) %>%
  arrange(desc(Importance)) %>%
  slice(1:20)

ggplot(rf_imp_normal_df, aes(x = reorder(Feature, Importance), y = Importance)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 20 Feature Importances: Random Forest - Normal",
    x = "Feature",
    y = "Importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

###  Variable Importance Plot for Tuned Random Forest

```{r rf-tuned-importance-plot, fig.height=7, fig.width=10}
rf_imp_tuned <- importance(final_tuned_rf)

rf_imp_tuned_df <- data.frame(
  Feature = rownames(rf_imp_tuned),
  Importance = rf_imp_tuned[, if ("%IncMSE" %in% colnames(rf_imp_tuned)) "%IncMSE" else 1]
) %>%
  arrange(desc(Importance)) %>%
  slice(1:20)

ggplot(rf_imp_tuned_df, aes(x = reorder(Feature, Importance), y = Importance)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Top 20 Feature Importances: Random Forest - Tuned",
    x = "Feature",
    y = "Importance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

###  Random Forest Tuning Results Plot

```{r rf-tuning-results-plot, fig.height=6, fig.width=10}
rf_tuning_results$Setting <- paste0(
  "mtry=", rf_tuning_results$mtry,
  ", ntree=", rf_tuning_results$ntree,
  ", nodesize=", rf_tuning_results$nodesize
)

ggplot(rf_tuning_results, aes(x = reorder(Setting, RMSE), y = RMSE)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Random Forest Tuning Results (Validation RMSE)",
    x = "Hyperparameter Setting",
    y = "Validation RMSE"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

##  Optional Tree Visual for Interpretation

This is **not the random forest itself**.  
It is a **single regression tree** trained only to give a tree-style visual interpretation, because a random forest contains many trees and cannot be summarized by one exact decision tree.

```{r illustrative-regression-tree, fig.height=7, fig.width=11}
reg_tree_model <- rpart(
  SalePrice_log ~ .,
  data = train_data,
  method = "anova"
)

rpart.plot::rpart.plot(
  reg_tree_model,
  main = "Illustrative Regression Tree (Not the Random Forest Model)"
)
```

##  Final Interpretation Notes

```{r final-result-note, results='asis'}
# Predictions on training data
pred_train_lm_normal <- predict(linear_model, newdata = train_data)
pred_train_lm_tuned  <- predict(final_tuned_lm, newdata = train_data)
pred_train_rf_normal <- predict(rf_model, newdata = train_data)
pred_train_rf_tuned  <- predict(final_tuned_rf, newdata = train_data)

# Predictions on test data
pred_test_lm_normal <- predict(linear_model, newdata = test_data)
pred_test_lm_tuned  <- predict(final_tuned_lm, newdata = test_data)
pred_test_rf_normal <- predict(rf_model, newdata = test_data)
pred_test_rf_tuned  <- predict(final_tuned_rf, newdata = test_data)

# Metrics function already assumed to exist:
# regression_metrics(actual, predicted)

# Training results
train_results_all <- rbind(
  cbind(Model = "Linear Regression - Normal", Dataset = "Train",
        regression_metrics(train_data$SalePrice, pred_train_lm_normal)),
  cbind(Model = "Linear Regression - Tuned", Dataset = "Train",
        regression_metrics(train_data$SalePrice, pred_train_lm_tuned)),
  cbind(Model = "Random Forest - Normal", Dataset = "Train",
        regression_metrics(train_data$SalePrice, pred_train_rf_normal)),
  cbind(Model = "Random Forest - Tuned", Dataset = "Train",
        regression_metrics(train_data$SalePrice, pred_train_rf_tuned))
)

# Testing results
test_results_all <- rbind(
  cbind(Model = "Linear Regression - Normal", Dataset = "Test",
        regression_metrics(test_data$SalePrice, pred_test_lm_normal)),
  cbind(Model = "Linear Regression - Tuned", Dataset = "Test",
        regression_metrics(test_data$SalePrice, pred_test_lm_tuned)),
  cbind(Model = "Random Forest - Normal", Dataset = "Test",
        regression_metrics(test_data$SalePrice, pred_test_rf_normal)),
  cbind(Model = "Random Forest - Tuned", Dataset = "Test",
        regression_metrics(test_data$SalePrice, pred_test_rf_tuned))
)

all_model_results <- rbind(train_results_all, test_results_all)

cat("Train and Test Metrics for All 4 Models:\n")
print(all_model_results)
```






```
